anycall_v6_router.role: Multichain anyCallV6 router on Ethereum. Held the locked-pool custody for the largest cross-chain transfers and verified the post-MPC aggregate signature before release. Modelled by MultichainAnyCallV6.sol extending the shared MockMultisig harness (N=1, K=1).

multichain_router_eth.role: Older Multichain Router (still active at the incident). Held additional ERC20 locked balances drained alongside the anyCallV6 router.

usdc_ethereum.role: USDC — ~$53M drained, the largest single asset.

mpc_signer_address.role: Aggregate signer that the on-chain code observed for cross-chain unlock authorisation. In production this address represented the post-MPC ceremony output; the corresponding TSS administration was de-facto unilateral and is the off-chain artifact that broke. Marked as is_eoa: true because the on-chain witness is a normal externally-owned-account-shaped key, not a contract.

attacker.role: Placeholder for the attacker EOA cluster that received the 6 large drain transactions over ~24 hours. Real attribution is unconfirmed as of this writing — successor staff or coercion against the CEO's contacts are the leading hypotheses (see ChainArgos timeline).

mpc_simulation.reason: Multichain's cross-chain authorisation in production used a Threshold Signature Scheme (TSS) / Multi-Party Computation (MPC) ceremony among a federated set of nodes. The ceremony produced ONE aggregate ECDSA signature per cross-chain action; the on-chain contract verified that single aggregate signature without seeing the partial signatures or the participating set. Solidity cannot verify TSS partial-signature protocols faithfully, so this benchmark **degrades to a 1-of-1 multi-sig** in which the lone signer represents the post-MPC aggregate output.
mpc_simulation.implication: The compromise model is 'the single administrator that controlled the MPC ceremony is now hostile' — equivalent to handing the lone signer's private key to the attacker. Module 1 should infer the same `validator_set_authority` invariant pattern as Ronin/Harmony (modulo N=1, K=1). Module 2's `mpc_compromise` / `key_compromise` scenarios violate the security argument by varying the off-chain administration dimension: who holds the post-aggregate key.
modelled_path: Production: federated MPC nodes -> TSS protocol -> aggregate ECDSA sig -> AnyCallV6Router.execute
Benchmark: single trusted signer -> ECDSA sig -> MultichainAnyCallV6.execute
Compromise: in production the federated trust collapsed to one administrator (Zhao Jun) before the incident; in the benchmark this is captured by 'the one configured signer key is in attacker hands'.
out_of_scope: Re-running the TSS ceremony with arbitrary participant sets (would require a cryptographic library outside Solidity's built-ins).
Modelling the MPC node liveness assumption that broke when the CEO was arrested (off-chain operational concern).

root_cause_summary.vulnerable_pattern: Bridge withdrawal authorisation requires one valid post-MPC-aggregate ECDSA signature. The on-chain check is correct.
root_cause_summary.verification_bypass: Off-chain: the TSS/MPC ceremony was de-facto unilaterally controlled by Multichain's CEO Zhao Jun, who was arrested in China in May 2023. The MPC nodes' keys could no longer be reconstituted by the team after the arrest. On 2023-07-06 the drains began, attributed by ChainArgos to either successor staff with surviving access or coercion against the CEO's contacts.
affected_logic: MultichainAnyCallV6._validateSignatures (modelled by MockMultisig.execute with N=1, K=1)
MPC ceremony administration (off-chain — not modelled in Solidity)
root_cause_summary.notes: Classified V2+V4 because: (a) V4 — the on-chain authority was a single aggregate key whose administrative compromise is the proximate cause; (b) V2 — the ceremony itself is a replay-style invariant in the sense that a single attacker observing the aggregate signature can re-sign arbitrary withdraw-receipt digests, replaying the ceremony's authority across many fraud transactions. The CEO's prior unilateral control means the federated assumption was always degenerate; the 2023-07-06 drains made it observable on-chain.

exploit_characteristics.style: MPC ceremony collapse via off-chain administrative compromise
exploit_characteristics.notes: Six large drain transactions issued over roughly 24 hours starting at block 17529624. Funds flowed cross-chain via the bridge's own routing into Fantom, Avalanche, and Polygon attacker accounts, then to mixer infrastructure. The Fantom Foundation publicly confirmed the incident the same day.

secondary_chains.name: avalanche
secondary_chains.note: Avalanche-side counterpart that received drained representations; smaller balance vs. Fantom main destination.

secondary_chains.name: polygon
secondary_chains.note: Polygon-side counterpart; targeted in the same 24-hour campaign.
